intent: Check whether the candidate understands Node.js event loop basics and can explain why asynchronous APIs matter for server responsiveness.
primaryQuestion: Explain the Node.js event loop at a practical level. What happens when an HTTP handler starts an asynchronous database call and returns control to Node?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that JavaScript execution is single-threaded while I/O is coordinated asynchronously by Node and the platform.

requirements.description: Explains that JavaScript handlers run on a single main event loop thread in a Node.js process.
Explains that database/network/file I/O is coordinated asynchronously by Node/libuv/OS or drivers rather than blocking JavaScript execution.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes the call stack from queued callbacks, timers, and promise continuations.

requirements.description: Distinguishes currently running call stack work from callbacks queued for later event loop turns.
Mentions timers, I/O callbacks, and promise continuations or microtasks as scheduled work.

expectedSignals.label: Connects non-blocking I/O to handling other requests while waiting for the database.

requirements.description: Explains that after starting an async database call, the handler can yield control back to the event loop.
Connects yielding during I/O waits to the process being able to start or continue handling other requests.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that CPU-heavy synchronous work can still block the process.

requirements.description: Mentions that CPU-heavy synchronous code blocks the event loop even if the app uses async I/O elsewhere.
Gives an example such as large JSON parsing, crypto, compression, image processing, regex, or a tight loop inside a request.

scoringHints.description: Explains event loop scheduling, async I/O, request concurrency, and blocking failure modes using a concrete server example.
Covers non-blocking I/O and callback scheduling but misses one detail such as microtasks or CPU blocking.
Knows Node is asynchronous but cannot connect it to server behavior.
Answer is too generic to validate practical event loop understanding.

intent: Evaluate senior incident leadership for a degraded Node.js service.
primaryQuestion: A new Node.js release causes intermittent 502s, event loop delay spikes, and queue backlog. How do you lead diagnosis, mitigation, and follow-up?

expectedSignals.label: Starts with impact, timeline, recent changes, and clear incident roles.

requirements.description: Defines user impact, timeline, affected routes/jobs/regions, recent release changes, and severity.
Defines roles such as incident lead, comms, investigation, mitigation owner, and scribe.

expectedSignals.label: Uses rollback, feature flags, traffic shaping, or worker scaling as mitigation options.

requirements.description: Compares rollback, disabling a feature flag, traffic shaping, load shedding, queue pausing, or worker scaling.
Chooses mitigation based on user recovery speed, data risk, confidence, and ability to verify improvement.

expectedSignals.label: Correlates gateway 502s, app logs, event loop delay, CPU, memory, and queue metrics.

requirements.description: Correlates gateway 502s with application logs, process restarts, request errors, and downstream failures.
Correlates event loop delay, CPU, memory, GC, queue depth, retry rate, and worker throughput.

expectedSignals.label: Defines post-incident actions with owners, tests, alerts, and prevention.

requirements.description: Defines post-incident actions with owners, deadlines, and verification criteria.
Adds tests, alerts, dashboards, runbooks, capacity changes, or code safeguards to prevent recurrence.

scoringHints.description: Shows incident command, evidence-based diagnosis, fast mitigation, stakeholder communication, and durable prevention.
Good technical debugging but misses communication, ownership, or follow-up depth.
Focuses only on local code debugging during a live incident.
No credible production incident approach.

followUps.question: At what point would you stop profiling and roll back?
Which metric tells you users are recovering after mitigation?

en.title: Lead a Node.js production incident
en.displayQuestion: A new Node.js release causes intermittent 502s, event loop delay spikes, and queue backlog. How do you lead diagnosis, mitigation, and follow-up?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate senior incident leadership and Node.js debugging.
guidance: Separate mitigation from root-cause work.
Include communication and prevention.
commonMistakes: Continuing investigation while impact grows.
Rolling back without follow-up ownership.

vi.title: Dẫn dắt incident production Node.js
vi.displayQuestion: Một release Node.js mới gây 502 lúc có lúc không, event loop delay spike và queue backlog. Bạn dẫn diagnosis, mitigation và follow-up thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá incident leadership và debugging Node.js ở mức senior.
guidance: Tách mitigation khỏi root-cause work.
Bao gồm communication và prevention.
commonMistakes: Tiếp tục điều tra khi impact đang tăng.
Rollback nhưng không có follow-up owner.

ja.title: Node.js production incident の lead
ja.displayQuestion: 新しい Node.js release が intermittent 502s、event loop delay spikes、queue backlog を起こしています。diagnosis、mitigation、follow-up をどう lead しますか。
ja.displayIntent: senior incident leadership と Node.js debugging を評価する。
guidance: mitigation と root-cause work を分ける。
communication と prevention を含める。
commonMistakes: impact が増える中で調査だけを続ける。
follow-up ownership なしに rollback で終わる。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Guide: Don't Block the Event Loop
sourceReferences.note: Used for event loop delay and blocking diagnosis framing.

sourceReferences.label: Google SRE Book: Managing Incidents
sourceReferences.note: Used for incident role, mitigation, and follow-up structure.

intent: Assess senior design for protecting Node.js APIs under abuse or overload.
primaryQuestion: Design rate limiting and load shedding for a high-traffic Node.js API. How do you avoid protecting the server in a way that unfairly breaks important users?

expectedSignals.label: Defines identity keys such as user, token, IP, tenant, or route.

requirements.description: Defines limiter keys using dimensions such as user, API token, IP, tenant, route, region, or plan.
Measures traffic distribution by identity so limits do not unfairly throttle important customers or shared NAT users.

expectedSignals.label: Compares fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, and concurrency limits at a high level.

requirements.description: Compares fixed window, sliding window, and token bucket/leaky bucket behavior for request-rate limits.
Explains when concurrency limits are better for expensive endpoints than simple requests-per-minute limits.

expectedSignals.label: Uses distributed storage such as Redis when multiple replicas are involved.

requirements.description: Uses Redis or another shared store when multiple Node.js replicas must enforce the same limit.
Handles atomic updates, latency, fail-open/fail-closed behavior, and fallback when the limiter store is degraded.

expectedSignals.label: Includes priority, quotas, retry-after responses, observability, and override mechanisms.

requirements.description: Supports customer tiers, quotas, allowlists, emergency overrides, and Retry-After or rate-limit response headers.
Monitors allowed/blocked rates, top limited identities, endpoint pressure, abuse signals, and organic customer spikes.

scoringHints.description: Designs fair distributed limits, overload behavior, client signaling, priority handling, and metrics.
Good rate-limit model but misses fairness, distributed state, or client behavior.
Knows rate limiting exists but designs a brittle global counter.
No viable overload protection design.

followUps.question: Which endpoints would you protect with concurrency limits instead of request-per-minute limits?
How would you tell the difference between abuse traffic and an organic customer spike?

en.title: Rate limiting and load shedding
en.displayQuestion: Design rate limiting and load shedding for a high-traffic Node.js API. How do you avoid protecting the server in a way that unfairly breaks important users?
en.displayIntent: Assess overload protection and fairness design.
guidance: Discuss identity, algorithms, and distributed state.
Include client-facing behavior and metrics.
commonMistakes: Using only local process counters.
Applying one global limit to every endpoint.

vi.title: Rate limiting và load shedding
vi.displayQuestion: Hãy thiết kế rate limiting và load shedding cho high-traffic Node.js API. Làm sao bạn bảo vệ server mà không vô tình làm hỏng trải nghiệm của user quan trọng?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá thiết kế overload protection và fairness.
guidance: Nói về identity, algorithm và distributed state.
Bao gồm behavior cho client và metrics.
commonMistakes: Chỉ dùng counter trong từng process.
Áp một global limit cho mọi endpoint.

ja.title: rate limiting と load shedding
ja.displayQuestion: high-traffic Node.js API の rate limiting と load shedding を設計してください。server を守る一方で重要な users を不公平に壊さないにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: overload protection と fairness design を評価する。
guidance: identity、algorithms、distributed state を説明する。
client-facing behavior と metrics を含める。
commonMistakes: local process counters だけを使う。
全 endpoint に一つの global limit を適用する。

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Denial of Service Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for resource exhaustion and rate limiting risk framing.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: HTTP
sourceReferences.note: Used for Node.js HTTP service behavior context.

intent: Verify senior ownership of a Node.js module-system or TypeScript runtime migration.
primaryQuestion: You mention migrating a Node.js service from CommonJS to ESM or changing the TypeScript runtime/build setup. What decisions were yours, what broke, and how did you prove the migration was safe?

expectedSignals.label: Separates personal technical decisions from team execution.

requirements.description: States which ESM, TypeScript runtime, build, testing, or deployment decisions the candidate personally owned.
Separates personal decisions from team execution, platform support, or dependency maintainer constraints.

expectedSignals.label: Explains package type, dependency interop, test tooling, build output, and deployment changes.

requirements.description: Explains package.json type, file extensions, import/require interop, and dependency compatibility changes.
Explains test runner, TypeScript compiler, bundler/runtime, build output, and deployment startup changes.

expectedSignals.label: Describes incremental rollout, compatibility shims, or rollback plan.

requirements.description: Describes incremental rollout, module-by-module conversion, compatibility shims, or dual-mode support.
Describes how the team could rollback, pin dependencies, revert build config, or recover if production broke.

expectedSignals.label: Provides evidence from tests, CI, production metrics, or reduced build/runtime issues.

requirements.description: Provides evidence from passing tests, CI stability, build reproducibility, dependency compatibility, or runtime smoke tests.
Provides production metrics or outcomes such as error rate, startup time, build time, incident rate, or reduced module-system issues.

scoringHints.description: Verifies personal ownership, technical migration details, breakages, rollout control, and measured success.
Real migration involvement but misses one dimension such as metrics or rollback.
Likely participated but cannot show senior decision ownership.
Claim remains unverifiable from the answer.

followUps.question: What baseline and final signal proved the migration improved or at least did not regress the service?

en.title: Verify Node.js ESM migration ownership
en.displayQuestion: You mention migrating a Node.js service from CommonJS to ESM or changing the TypeScript runtime/build setup. What decisions were yours, what broke, and how did you prove the migration was safe?
en.displayIntent: Verify senior ownership of a Node.js module/build migration.
guidance: Separate your decisions from team work.
Include breakages, rollout, and evidence.
commonMistakes: Using only 'we migrated' language.
Skipping compatibility and rollback details.

vi.title: Xác minh ownership migration ESM Node.js
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn nói mình migrate một Node.js service từ CommonJS sang ESM hoặc đổi TypeScript runtime/build setup. Quyết định nào là của bạn, thứ gì đã vỡ, và bạn chứng minh migration an toàn thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Xác minh ownership senior trong migration module/build Node.js.
guidance: Tách quyết định của bạn khỏi công việc của team.
Bao gồm breakage, rollout và bằng chứng.
commonMistakes: Chỉ nói 'chúng tôi migrate'.
Bỏ qua compatibility và rollback detail.

ja.title: Node.js ESM migration ownership の確認
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js service を CommonJS から ESM へ移行した、または TypeScript runtime/build setup を変えたとあります。どの判断があなたのもので、何が壊れ、migration が安全だとどう証明しましたか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js module/build migration の senior ownership を確認する。
guidance: 自分の判断と team work を分ける。
breakages、rollout、evidence を含める。
commonMistakes: 「we migrated」だけで話す。
compatibility と rollback details を省略する。

sourceReferences.note: Used for ESM behavior, package settings, and interoperability context.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: Modules - CommonJS
sourceReferences.note: Used for CommonJS migration context.

intent: Assess advanced worker thread pool design and context propagation.
primaryQuestion: If you build a worker thread pool for CPU-heavy work in Node.js, how do you manage pool size, cancellation, errors, memory transfer, and trace context?

expectedSignals.label: Sizes the pool based on CPU, workload, and co-location with the main service.

requirements.description: Sizes the worker pool based on CPU cores, task cost, service co-location, and expected concurrency.
Avoids oversubscribing CPU so worker tasks do not starve the event loop or other processes on the same host.

expectedSignals.label: Uses bounded queues, timeouts, cancellation or abort signals where possible.

requirements.description: Uses a bounded task queue or admission control to avoid unbounded memory growth.
Uses timeouts, cancellation, abort signals, or cooperative cancellation where possible.

expectedSignals.label: Handles worker crashes, task errors, retries, and backpressure to callers.

requirements.description: Handles worker exits, crashes, task exceptions, and replacement without losing caller-visible errors.
Applies backpressure, queue rejection, retry policy, or fallback when the pool is saturated.

expectedSignals.label: Understands structured clone, TransferList, SharedArrayBuffer trade-offs, and context propagation limitations.

requirements.description: Compares structured clone copying, TransferList ownership transfer, and SharedArrayBuffer/shared memory trade-offs.
Explains that AsyncLocalStorage context does not automatically cross workers and request IDs/trace context must be passed explicitly.

scoringHints.description: Designs bounded worker pool behavior with resource sizing, cancellation, memory transfer, errors, and observability.
Good worker pool design but misses context propagation or memory-transfer nuance.
Knows workers can do CPU work but designs unsafe unbounded execution.
No credible advanced worker-thread design.

followUps.question: When would you transfer an ArrayBuffer instead of copying it?
How would logs from a worker still include the original request ID?

en.title: Worker thread pool design
en.displayQuestion: If you build a worker thread pool for CPU-heavy work in Node.js, how do you manage pool size, cancellation, errors, memory transfer, and trace context?
en.displayIntent: Assess advanced worker thread pool design.
guidance: Discuss bounded queues and resource sizing.
Include memory transfer and observability.
commonMistakes: Creating unbounded workers.
Losing trace context across worker boundaries.

vi.title: Thiết kế worker thread pool
vi.displayQuestion: Nếu bạn xây worker thread pool cho CPU-heavy work trong Node.js, bạn quản lý pool size, cancellation, error, memory transfer và trace context thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá thiết kế worker thread pool nâng cao.
guidance: Nói về bounded queue và resource sizing.
Bao gồm memory transfer và observability.
commonMistakes: Tạo worker không giới hạn.
Mất trace context qua worker boundary.

ja.title: worker thread pool design
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js で CPU-heavy work 用の worker thread pool を作るなら、pool size、cancellation、errors、memory transfer、trace context をどう管理しますか。
ja.displayIntent: advanced worker thread pool design を評価する。
guidance: bounded queues と resource sizing を説明する。
memory transfer と observability を含める。
commonMistakes: unbounded workers を作る。
worker boundaries で trace context を失う。

sourceReferences.note: Used for worker lifecycle, messaging, transfer, and AsyncResource considerations.

sourceReferences.note: Used for async context and worker pool diagnostics context.

intent: Evaluate senior architecture judgment for a growing Node.js codebase.
primaryQuestion: A Node.js monolith has grown into many tightly coupled modules with slow releases. How would you decide whether to modularize internally, split services, or keep the monolith and improve boundaries?

expectedSignals.label: Starts from product, team, deployment, data ownership, and operational constraints.

requirements.description: Starts with product boundaries, team ownership, release cadence, deployment needs, and operational maturity.
Identifies data ownership, transaction boundaries, consistency needs, and shared database constraints.

expectedSignals.label: Compares internal modularization, service extraction, and monolith hardening.

requirements.description: Compares internal modularization or modular monolith hardening as a lower-operational-cost option.
Compares service extraction benefits and costs such as independent deploys, network contracts, operations, and data split complexity.

expectedSignals.label: Considers API contracts, database boundaries, transactions, observability, and rollback.

requirements.description: Defines module/service API contracts, database ownership boundaries, and transaction/consistency implications.
Plans tracing, logs, metrics, rollback, compatibility, and failure handling across new boundaries.

expectedSignals.label: Defines incremental migration, ownership, and success metrics.

requirements.description: Defines an incremental migration plan, module/service ownership, governance, and rollout order.
Defines baseline and target metrics such as release frequency, lead time, incidents, defect rate, latency, deployment failures, or team coordination cost.

scoringHints.description: Balances architecture options, data and deployment boundaries, team ownership, migration risk, and measurable outcomes.
Good architecture reasoning but misses one operational dimension such as rollback or observability.
Defaults to microservices or monolith without evaluating constraints.
No senior-level architecture decision process.

followUps.question: Which module would you extract first, and what risk would make you reject extraction?
What baseline and target metrics would prove the architecture change helped?

en.title: Node.js monolith boundaries
en.displayQuestion: A Node.js monolith has grown into many tightly coupled modules with slow releases. How would you decide whether to modularize internally, split services, or keep the monolith and improve boundaries?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate senior architecture trade-off judgment.
guidance: Compare realistic options.
Include data ownership, rollout, and metrics.
commonMistakes: Choosing microservices by default.
Ignoring database and transaction coupling.

vi.title: Boundary của Node.js monolith
vi.displayQuestion: Một Node.js monolith đã lớn thành nhiều module tightly coupled và release chậm. Bạn quyết định modularize nội bộ, tách service, hoặc giữ monolith và cải thiện boundary thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá judgment architecture trade-off ở mức senior.
guidance: So sánh các lựa chọn thực tế.
Bao gồm data ownership, rollout và metric.
commonMistakes: Mặc định chọn microservices.
Bỏ qua database và transaction coupling.

ja.title: Node.js monolith boundaries
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js monolith が多くの tightly coupled modules に成長し、release が遅くなっています。internal modularization、service split、monolith を維持して boundaries 改善のどれを選ぶかどう判断しますか。
ja.displayIntent: senior architecture trade-off judgment を評価する。
guidance: 現実的な options を比較する。
data ownership、rollout、metrics を含める。
commonMistakes: default で microservices を選ぶ。
